speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome back. Today we're working on two layers of open source that beginners often underestimate: culture and organization. In other words, how people work together, and how work gets sorted so collaboration stays manageable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this is not just about writing code.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Technical skill matters, but open source is also a social system. Communication quality affects whether people can participate, whether reviews feel safe, and whether issues and pull requests move forward.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for our learners, that includes accessible participation too.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. Accessibility is not only about the final app or website. It also includes whether issues are readable, whether labels are understandable, whether saved replies reduce typing burden, whether a screen reader user can find the right controls, and whether people describe their reasoning clearly in public threads.

speaker: ALEX
text: A quick workshop note before we go deeper. In this chapter, the challenge is a guided reflection. There's no bot grading because communication quality is too subjective for fair automation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's refreshing.

speaker: ALEX
text: It is. The evidence is your reflection comment on your assigned challenge issue. The pattern is simple: read, reflect, and commit to one or more specific behaviors.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What are learners actually asked to do?

speaker: ALEX
text: They reflect on questions like: what made you feel welcome, what would make the learning room more accessible or inclusive, or what they learned from a peer simulation issue, pull request, or classmate. Then they practice triage by finding an open issue, adding at least one label, and optionally tagging a specific person if the issue needs that person's attention.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So it blends reflection with action.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. And the guided reflection version asks for concrete behaviors, not vague intentions. For example: one respectful review habit you will use, one way you will ask for help clearly, and one way you will respond to feedback constructively.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Not "I'll be nice," but something observable.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Better examples sound like: "I'll start review comments by acknowledging something that works," or "I'll describe the exact step where I got stuck and what I already tried."

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if someone gets stuck writing the reflection?

speaker: ALEX
text: Use one simple sentence per prompt. Draft in a text editor first if that feels easier. Focus on one real behavior you can start now. Ask a facilitator for an example if needed. And if you finish but feel unsure, compare against the reference solution.

speaker: ALEX
text: That reference solution matters because it shows what counts as success.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What does it emphasize?

speaker: ALEX
text: That open source projects are communities, not just code repositories. Reading files like CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md and CONTRIBUTING.md can change how you see participation. A good reflection notices that filing a clear issue, structuring docs well, or communicating access needs are real contributions.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I like that. It expands the idea of contribution.

speaker: ALEX
text: It also points out that accessibility affects the development process itself. The way we write issues, name branches, structure documentation, and discuss changes can either include or exclude people.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the solution includes triage too, right?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. For example, if an issue says a welcome page loads slowly on mobile, a strong recommendation is the label bug, because the behavior is not working as expected. An additional label might be accessibility, because slow reloads can disproportionately burden users of assistive technology.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So good triage includes justification, not just a guess.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. The point is thoughtful reasoning. Alternate approaches are also valid: reflecting on the code of conduct, the contributing guide, or comparing one project's norms to another. What matters is understanding that open source has cultural norms, not just technical rules.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now let's put those conversations in workflow context. Most open source contribution in this workshop follows GitHub Flow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's the lightweight model centered on pull requests, right?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Six steps. Create a branch from main with a descriptive name. Make your changes and commit in small logical units. Open a pull request early, even as a draft if needed. Discuss and review collaboratively. Pass status checks like tests or linting. Then a maintainer merges into main.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Why is this model so common?

speaker: ALEX
text: Because it keeps main deployable, treats branches as cheap and temporary, makes pull requests the unit of conversation, and encourages small changes that are easier to review.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That "pull requests are the unit of conversation" idea feels important.

speaker: ALEX
text: It is. The why, the tradeoffs, the linked issue, the feedback, and the approval all live together. That's especially helpful when work is asynchronous and public.

speaker: JAMIE
text: You mentioned GitHub Flow. Some learners will also hear about Git Flow. What's the difference?

speaker: ALEX
text: Git Flow is older and more complex. It usually has long-lived main and develop branches, plus temporary feature, release, and hotfix branches. Feature work branches off develop, release branches collect work for scheduled releases, and hotfixes branch from main for urgent production fixes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So Git Flow fits projects with planned release cycles.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Enterprise products, mobile apps with app store review, embedded systems, or older projects with versioned releases. GitHub Flow is simpler: usually just main plus short-lived branches, with each merge to main expected to be deployable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And this workshop uses GitHub Flow because it's better for open source contribution and fast-moving web work.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Lower complexity, easier onboarding, and fewer branching rules to memorize.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There's also an unwritten rule here, isn't there?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes: one thing per branch. One fix, one feature, one documentation change. That makes commit history cleaner, reviews narrower, and rework less painful. If a branch tries to solve three unrelated problems, reviewers struggle and authors get tangled.

speaker: ALEX
text: Another practical habit is keeping your fork up to date.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Because if your fork drifts too far from upstream, you inherit old problems and more merge conflicts.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Syncing reduces conflicts, keeps you working from current code, and avoids opening pull requests based on stale main.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What are the main ways to sync?

speaker: ALEX
text: Three common ones. First, the GitHub web interface, which is often the easiest. If GitHub shows a sync option on your fork's main branch, you can update from the browser. Second, the command line in a terminal, including the VS Code terminal. One-time setup: add the upstream remote, usually the original repository. After that, the sync process is: switch to your own main branch, fetch upstream changes, merge upstream main into your main, then push the updated main to your fork on GitHub.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can you say those steps cleanly one more time?

speaker: ALEX
text: Sure. One: switch to main. Two: fetch upstream. Three: merge upstream's main into your local main. Four: push your updated main to origin, meaning your fork.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the third method?

speaker: ALEX
text: GitHub Desktop, if that's your preferred interface.

speaker: JAMIE
text: When should people sync?

speaker: ALEX
text: Before starting new work, before creating a new branch, if your pull request shows conflicts, and any time the upstream repo has moved significantly since your last update.

speaker: ALEX
text: Next: commit messages. These are tiny pieces of communication, but they carry a lot of weight.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What makes a commit message good?

speaker: ALEX
text: A clear first line is required. It should briefly state what changed. The body is optional and can explain why, context, or tradeoffs. A footer is also optional for references like issue numbers or special notes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the first line is the must-have.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Keep it specific and meaningful. "Fix broken README link" is useful. "Stuff" is not.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And atomic commits?

speaker: ALEX
text: That means each commit should represent one coherent, complete change. Not half a fix, not an unrelated bundle. Atomic commits make review easier, make rollback safer, and help future readers understand the history.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Common mistakes?

speaker: ALEX
text: Messages that are too vague, too long, or trying to describe multiple unrelated changes at once. Another common mistake is using commits as a dumping ground instead of making deliberate checkpoints.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So a good commit message is part clarity, part respect for your future collaborators.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now let's zoom out to the nature of open source communication. It is usually in writing, asynchronous, and public.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Each of those changes how we should communicate.

speaker: ALEX
text: They do. In writing means your words may be read without tone of voice or facial cues, so clarity matters. Asynchronously means people respond later, possibly in different time zones, so your message should stand on its own. In public means others may learn from the thread later, so context and professionalism matter even when you are talking to one person.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That explains why vague comments feel extra costly online.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The reader cannot interrupt in real time to ask what you meant.

speaker: ALEX
text: A very useful structure for feedback has five parts. First, acknowledge what's working.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That lowers defensiveness and shows you actually read the work.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Second, identify the specific concern. Be precise. Third, explain why it matters. Fourth, suggest a path forward when you can. And fifth, signal the weight of the concern.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Meaning say whether it's required, optional, blocking, or just an idea.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Not every comment has equal importance. If you don't signal weight, authors may treat preferences like hard requirements or ignore serious issues by mistake.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Could that sound like, "Non-blocking suggestion," versus, "Required before merge because this breaks keyboard navigation"?

speaker: ALEX
text: Perfect example.

speaker: ALEX
text: Language and tone matter too. Prefer "we" language or describe the code rather than the person.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So instead of "You wrote this wrong," say something like, "This function may fail when the input is empty," or "Could we handle the empty case here?"

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Use tentative language when you're uncertain: "I think," "It looks like," "Could this be." Acknowledge cultural and language diversity, because not everyone shares the same idioms, confidence norms, or first language. And avoid urgency markers unless something is truly urgent.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Because "ASAP!!!" creates stress and often hides weak reasoning.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. If it is genuinely urgent, say why.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's talk commenting etiquette. Keep comments focused.

speaker: JAMIE
text: One comment per concern when possible?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Focused comments are easier to resolve and discuss. Also, don't leave comments unresolved without intention. Use conversation resolution thoughtfully.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Meaning if a concern was addressed, resolve it. If not, don't quietly move on.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. And do not pile on. If someone already made the same point, you usually don't need to repeat it. Add a reaction or briefly note agreement if useful.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That helps reduce noise.

speaker: ALEX
text: It does. Reactions are part of etiquette too. A thumbs up can acknowledge agreement without forcing the author to read five duplicate comments.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There's also a really practical accessibility trick here: saved replies.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, and it's one of my favorite workflow improvements. Saved replies let you reuse common comment text. That reduces repetitive typing, supports consistency, and can lower cognitive and physical effort.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Common uses?

speaker: ALEX
text: Thank-you notes, review templates, requests for missing information, accessibility reminders, testing instructions, and standard explanations like "Please link the related issue" or "Could you add reproduction steps?"

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you create one?

speaker: ALEX
text: In GitHub settings, create a saved reply with a name and body. Then when writing a comment, insert it from the comment box tools.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And using it in a comment is basically choosing the saved reply and then customizing as needed.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Never let templates make you sound robotic. Use them as a starting point.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Any screen reader guidance?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Learn the path to the saved replies control in the comment editor and practice it once when you're not under time pressure. That turns it into a reliable accessibility win later.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now, code review etiquette for reviewers. Review the code, not the person. Don't gatekeep knowledge. Ask questions instead of making demands when appropriate. Distinguish opinion from requirement. And when the work is ready, approve explicitly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's unpack "don't gatekeep knowledge."

speaker: ALEX
text: If a contributor didn't know something, explain it or point to the resource. Don't act as though the lack of knowledge disqualifies them from participating.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And "distinguish opinion from requirement" means separating style preferences from actual project policy or correctness issues.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. "I prefer this variable name" is different from "This violates the documented API contract."

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about authors receiving review?

speaker: ALEX
text: Say thank you. Don't take feedback personally. Explain your choices when needed. And surface blockers early.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if you're stuck applying requested changes, don't disappear.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Say what is blocking you: a merge conflict, uncertainty about the request, lack of permissions, failed tests, or needing clarification.

speaker: ALEX
text: There's also a specific topic here: inclusive commenting around accessibility issues.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Meaning don't frame accessibility as a niche preference or optional extra.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Describe the barrier and its impact. Focus on users and outcomes. Avoid language that minimizes the issue. And when possible, connect the feedback to standards, keyboard access, screen reader behavior, color contrast, focus order, meaningful alt text, or whatever concrete concern applies.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So accessible feedback is precise, respectful, and grounded.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes.

speaker: ALEX
text: Another subtle idea is the social contract behind the label "good first issue."

speaker: JAMIE
text: That label is more than decoration.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. It suggests the issue is scoped appropriately for a newcomer, the problem is reasonably described, and maintainers will be responsive enough that a beginner is not abandoned.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if a project labels something "good first issue" but it actually needs deep architecture knowledge, that breaks trust.

speaker: ALEX
text: Well said.

speaker: ALEX
text: Open source also includes difficult moments. If you receive harsh feedback, pause before replying, extract the technical point if there is one, and respond calmly or seek help. If you disagree with a decision, ask for clarification and reasoning instead of escalating immediately.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if someone is rude to you?

speaker: ALEX
text: Document what happened, disengage if needed, and use the project's code of conduct or moderation channels. You do not have to tolerate abuse to be a "good contributor."

speaker: JAMIE
text: What if you realize you caused offense?

speaker: ALEX
text: Acknowledge it directly, apologize without defensiveness, and change the behavior. Don't turn the conversation into a debate about your intentions.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's shift to documentation. Writing your first README is one of the most beginner-friendly and high-value contributions.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What belongs in a README?

speaker: ALEX
text: At minimum: project name, a short explanation of what the project is, installation steps if relevant, usage, contribution guidance, and license information.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So a simple skeleton might be project name, installation, usage, contributing, and license.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. That skeleton is a strong starting point. A good README helps new users and contributors understand what the project does, how to run it, how to help, and what rules apply.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What makes a README accessible?

speaker: ALEX
text: Clear headings, meaningful link text, readable structure, alt text for images when appropriate, not relying on screenshots alone for essential instructions, and keeping prose concise and scannable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And good versus bad?

speaker: ALEX
text: A good README answers basic newcomer questions quickly. A bad README is vague, outdated, missing setup steps, or visually flashy but structurally weak for screen reader users.

speaker: ALEX
text: Related to that are community health files. These are the governance and guidance files that help people participate responsibly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's name them.

speaker: ALEX
text: CONTRIBUTING.md explains how to contribute. CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md describes expected behavior and reporting paths. SECURITY.md tells people how to report security vulnerabilities safely. LICENSE defines legal reuse rights. And you should know how to find these files on GitHub, because they often answer questions before you need to ask them.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So before contributing, reading these files is both polite and strategic.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Another important skill is choosing the right communication channel.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Public issue, pull request comment, discussion, direct contact, security report--those aren't interchangeable.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Use issues for trackable work and bugs, pull requests for code-specific review, discussions when a repository enables broader conversation, and private security channels for vulnerabilities. Match the channel to the purpose and privacy needs.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There was also a quick-reference idea in the chapter: phrases that work.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Practical phrases like: "I may be missing context, but…", "Could we clarify…", "Non-blocking suggestion: …", "Thanks, this part is especially clear," and "I'm stuck at step three; I tried A and B." These are small wording choices that make collaboration easier.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And learners are encouraged to rewrite one comment as practice.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Turning a blunt or vague comment into one that is specific, respectful, and actionable is a great exercise.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now let's widen the lens to first-time contribution in general. Open source means source code and project materials are available for people to inspect, use, and often modify under a license.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Who can contribute?

speaker: ALEX
text: Anyone. Not only expert programmers. Documentation writers, testers, designers, translators, accessibility reviewers, issue triagers, and people who improve examples or reporting all contribute.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What makes a good first contribution?

speaker: ALEX
text: Something small, clear, and contained. Maybe a documentation fix, typo, broken link, simple bug reproduction clarification, or a focused accessibility improvement.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And signs it's too large for a first attempt?

speaker: ALEX
text: The issue is vague, touches many files, requires architecture decisions, has unclear acceptance criteria, or depends on unfinished background work.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do people find something to work on?

speaker: ALEX
text: Look for labels like good first issue, documentation, help wanted, or accessibility. Read the repository's README and contribution guide. Search issues with clear scope. And notice whether maintainers seem active and responsive.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then read the issue carefully before starting.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Understand the problem, expected behavior, current discussion, linked files, and whether someone is already working on it.

speaker: ALEX
text: The basic contribution workflow is often: fork the repository, clone your fork locally if you're working locally, create a branch, edit files, commit your changes, push, and open a pull request.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That connects back to GitHub Flow.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. And your pull request description matters. Explain what changed, why, how to test it, and link the related issue when appropriate.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Such as using a closing keyword if the PR should close an issue when merged.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. We'll come back to that in the cross-reference section.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about getting help?

speaker: ALEX
text: Ask clear questions. Include what you expected, what happened instead, what step you were on, and what you already tried. That's more helpful than "It doesn't work."

speaker: JAMIE
text: And after your contribution is merged?

speaker: ALEX
text: Celebrate, but also keep learning. Read feedback, note what reviewers focused on, maybe thank maintainers, and consider a second contribution while the workflow is still fresh.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's how you build a contribution habit.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Practical habits include making small contributions regularly, reading project docs before asking, keeping branches focused, and leaving the project slightly clearer than you found it.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now we move into the organizational layer: labels, milestones, projects, and cross-references.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This is the part that turns a messy issue list into something navigable.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. And there's a workshop challenge here too. Learners read an open issue and post a structured triage recommendation that a maintainer could act on immediately.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What's in that recommendation?

speaker: ALEX
text: Four fields: suggested labels, suggested milestone, suggested project board column, and a one-sentence reason. If the learner has write access, they can apply the labels and milestone directly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if they don't have permissions?

speaker: ALEX
text: They can still do valuable triage. Triage is about clarity, not authority.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I love that line.

speaker: ALEX
text: It's true. A clear recommendation saves maintainers time even if you cannot click the controls yourself.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There's also an agentic angle mentioned in the material.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Labels and issue states can drive automation or agents. For example, an agent might only activate when an issue gets a specific label like needs-review or accessibility-check.

speaker: ALEX
text: So, labels. They are colored tags on issues and pull requests that communicate category, priority, status, or other metadata at a glance.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for screen reader users, those labels are announced with the issue title.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. You may hear "Label: bug" or "Labels: accessibility, good first issue." That auditory context helps scanning.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What standard labels should beginners recognize?

speaker: ALEX
text: Common ones include bug, enhancement, documentation, good first issue, help wanted, question, invalid, wontfix, duplicate, accessibility, and needs triage.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you get to the labels page?

speaker: ALEX
text: From a repository, go to the Issues tab and find the Labels link near Milestones in the toolbar. For screen reader users, that may mean navigating to the Issues tab and using link navigation to find Labels. On macOS with VoiceOver, quick nav or rotor navigation can help. On Windows with NVDA or JAWS, heading or link shortcuts can help.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And in the terminal?

speaker: ALEX
text: Using GitHub CLI, you can list all labels with descriptions using gh label list, or output structured data with JSON fields like name and description.

speaker: ALEX
text: Applying labels can happen in several environments. In the browser, open the issue or PR, find the Labels control in the right sidebar, activate it, and select one or more labels. In VS Code with the GitHub Pull Requests extension, you can manage labels from the GitHub panel. GitHub Desktop doesn't directly manage labels, so you usually bounce to the browser. In GitHub CLI, you can edit an issue or PR and add labels from the command line.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should screen reader users expect when using the browser control?

speaker: ALEX
text: Usually a button or gear next to Labels in the sidebar. Activating it opens a dropdown where labels can be navigated, filtered by typing, selected or deselected, and then closed with Escape. Selections save automatically.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can you add multiple labels at once?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. You can add more than one, remove labels, and apply labels to either issues or pull requests.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And filtering by label is where labels really become useful.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. You can list issues with a specific label, combine multiple labels, combine label filters with state like open or closed, and search across labels to find patterns.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can repos create custom labels too?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. A maintainer can create a new label, give it a name, description, and color. Color can be meaningful visually, but the text name and description matter most for accessibility.

speaker: ALEX
text: Next are milestones. A milestone groups issues or pull requests toward a larger target, often a release, sprint, phase, or event.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if labels classify the type of work, milestones group work by shared destination.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. You can navigate to Milestones from the Issues area. Reading a milestone typically tells you its title, due date if any, description, and progress, such as how many items are open or closed.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What does opening a milestone mean here?

speaker: ALEX
text: Creating or viewing an active milestone for current work. Then individual issues can be assigned to that milestone or removed from it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And with GitHub CLI or the web, you can list issues in a milestone too.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. That helps you see what belongs to a shared goal.

speaker: ALEX
text: Cross-references are another small but powerful feature.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Meaning linking issues and pull requests to each other?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Types of cross-references include mentioning an issue number, linking from a pull request to an issue, or using closing keywords like "Closes #42."

speaker: JAMIE
text: And when does the closing keyword actually fire?

speaker: ALEX
text: When the pull request is merged into the relevant branch. Merely opening the PR does not close the issue. The merge triggers the close.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's an important nuance.

speaker: ALEX
text: It is. Good cross-references make work traceable. They connect the problem discussion to the proposed fix.

speaker: ALEX
text: Then there are GitHub Projects, which are planning and tracking spaces that can show work in table or board views.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should a blind or low-vision learner know about navigating them?

speaker: ALEX
text: First, know where to find a project from the repository or organization. In table view, think in rows and fields. A screen reader may announce the item title plus row-related details depending on focus. In board view, think in columns and cards, similar to To Do, In Progress, Done, or Needs Triage.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So table view may be more structured for some users, while board view may mirror workflow stages.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. And issues can be added to a project so they appear in that planning system alongside metadata.

speaker: ALEX
text: For the hackathon, the materials suggest a practical organization strategy.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let me guess: create a small useful label set, maybe a milestone, and follow a simple workflow.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's it. Labels to create might include documentation, bug, accessibility, enhancement, and good first issue. A milestone could represent the hackathon or a workshop phase. The workflow is to inspect new issues, label them, decide whether they belong in the milestone, and place them in the right project column such as Needs Triage, To Do, or In Progress.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So not overengineering. Just enough structure to make the work visible.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's tie this back to the triage challenge. A strong triage recommendation might sound like this: issue number, suggested labels documentation and accessibility, suggested milestone hackathon day one, suggested project column Needs Triage, one-sentence reason explaining that the issue is a docs fix affecting access and should be reviewed before active implementation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if milestone or project placement is unclear?

speaker: ALEX
text: Say none and explain why, or use Needs Triage as the safe default. Uncertainty stated clearly is better than pretending confidence.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's a nice general rule for open source communication too.

speaker: ALEX
text: It is.

speaker: ALEX
text: Before we close, let's revisit the challenge logistics from both chapters in one place. For the culture challenge, you may be asked to share a reflection in the peer simulation issue or with a buddy. Different perspectives enrich the community. If you don't see labels, you may need to create them or ask a facilitator to set up defaults. If you can't label someone else's issue, that may be a permissions issue, not a personal mistake.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if you don't know what to reflect on, start simple: what is one thing you learned today?

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. There are no wrong answers if the reflection is thoughtful. For the organization challenge, if you're unsure which label to pick, start with one of documentation, bug, or accessibility. If the milestone is unclear, say none with a reason. If the board column is unknown, Needs Triage is a valid default.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And both challenges use a similar philosophy: explain your reasoning clearly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Reflection and triage are both forms of visible thinking.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can we end with a few behaviors learners can carry into every repository they touch?

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. Keep branches focused: one thing per branch. Sync your fork before starting new work. Write clear commit messages. Read the README, CONTRIBUTING, CODE_OF_CONDUCT, SECURITY, and LICENSE files. In review, acknowledge what works, be specific about concerns, explain why they matter, and signal whether they're blocking. Use respectful, low-assumption language. Don't pile on duplicate comments. Use reactions and saved replies wisely. Label and link issues so work is easier to find. And remember that accessibility includes the collaboration process, not just the shipped product.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That last point really changes how you see open source.

speaker: ALEX
text: It does. The best projects do not only welcome contributions in theory. They make participation understandable, navigable, and humane.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And that's good culture and good engineering.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.
